title: 16:9 Aspect Ratio Calculator - Screen & Display Dimensions
tagline: Calculate 16:9 screen dimensions, diagonal size, pixel density, and area for TVs, monitors, and laptops.
intro: Enter width, height, and optionally a diagonal size to calculate all 16:9 display properties including pixel density and area.

error.invalidInput: Please enter valid positive numbers for width and height.

about.heading: About the 16:9 Aspect Ratio Calculator
about.body:
The 16:9 aspect ratio is the standard widescreen format that now dominates consumer electronics. Its width-to-height ratio of approximately 1.778 (16 divided by 9) was chosen as an international standard for high-definition television (HDTV) in the early 1990s and has since become the default for monitors, laptops, smartphones in landscape mode, online video, and digital cinema cropping. Understanding this ratio is essential for anyone working with video production, display hardware, user interface design, or photography.

The 16:9 standard was a deliberate mathematical compromise. Early widescreen cinema used ratios like 1.85:1 and 2.39:1, while traditional television used the squarer 4:3 (1.333). The SMPTE committee that standardised HDTV chose 16:9 because it is the geometric mean of 4:3 and 2.39:1, meaning a single screen at 16:9 can best accommodate both standard-def content and widescreen cinema with the least letterboxing or pillarboxing. The ratio was formalised in ITU-R BT.709 and later ITU-R BT.2020.

Common 16:9 pixel resolutions span every tier of quality. The 1280×720 (720p HD) resolution launched consumer HDTV. The 1920×1080 (1080p Full HD) format is still the most widely used resolution for monitors, laptops, and broadcast content. The 2560×1440 (1440p or QHD/WQHD) format is popular in gaming monitors. The 3840×2160 (4K UHD) format is standard for modern televisions and premium monitors. The 7680×4320 (8K) format is emerging in high-end broadcasting and cinema production. All of these divide to exactly 16/9 ≈ 1.7778.

This calculator lets you verify that a given width and height conform to the 16:9 ratio, compute the diagonal size via the Pythagorean theorem, and — when working in pixels with a known physical diagonal — calculate the pixel density in pixels per inch (PPI). PPI is the key measure of screen sharpness: a 1920×1080 panel on a 27-inch monitor has a PPI of about 82, while the same resolution on a 15.6-inch laptop screen produces about 141 PPI and appears noticeably sharper.

The area output (width × height) is useful for comparing display surface sizes across different diagonals and resolutions, computing coverage in physical units for printed materials, or checking that a video crop or graphic maintains the correct proportions. Whether you are specifying hardware, designing a UI layout, verifying a video export setting, or simply comparing television sizes, this calculator gives you all the key 16:9 measurements at once.

examples.heading: 16:9 Aspect Ratio Examples
examples.intro: Common 16:9 displays from televisions, monitors, and laptops with resolutions and pixel densities.

items.note: Full HD at 92 PPI. Everyday desktop monitor resolution.
4K UHD at 80 PPI. Standard large-screen living room TV.
Full HD at 141 PPI. Common mid-range laptop screen.
1440p QHD at 109 PPI. Popular gaming monitor resolution.

howto.heading: How to Use the 16:9 Aspect Ratio Calculator
steps: Select the unit for your measurements — pixels, centimetres, inches, or millimetres.
Enter the width of the display in the Width field.
Enter the height of the display in the Height field.
Optionally enter the physical diagonal size (in the same unit) to calculate pixel density (PPI) when using pixels.
Click Calculate to see the aspect ratio check, diagonal, area, and pixel density. Click Reset to clear all fields.

items.a: The 16:9 aspect ratio means the display is 16 units wide for every 9 units tall. Dividing width by height gives approximately 1.7778. It is the universal standard for HDTV, online video, computer monitors, and most laptops introduced since 2010.

The SMPTE committee chose 16:9 because it is the geometric mean of the two dominant professional aspect ratios at the time — 4:3 (television) and 2.39:1 (widescreen cinema). A single 16:9 screen can display both formats with the least wasted space. It was formally standardised in ITU-R BT.709 for HDTV.

items.q: What are the most common 16:9 resolutions?
items.a: The main 16:9 resolutions are 1280×720 (720p HD), 1920×1080 (1080p Full HD), 2560×1440 (1440p QHD), 3840×2160 (4K UHD), and 7680×4320 (8K). Each divides exactly to 16/9. The 1920×1080 and 3840×2160 resolutions are the most widely used today.

items.q: How do I calculate the diagonal of a 16:9 display?
items.a: Use the Pythagorean theorem: diagonal = √(width² + height²). For a 1920×1080 display: √(1920² + 1080²) = √(3686400 + 1166400) = √4852800 ≈ 2203 pixels. Divide by the PPI to get the physical size in inches.

items.q: What is a good PPI for a monitor?
items.a: For a desktop monitor viewed at arm's length, 90–120 PPI is generally sharp enough. For laptops at closer viewing distances, 140–200 PPI is ideal. Above 220 PPI, individual pixels are typically invisible to the naked eye at normal viewing distances, which is the threshold Apple uses for 'Retina' displays.

items.q: How do I convert a 4:3 video to 16:9?
items.a: There are two approaches. Letterboxing adds black bars on the left and right sides to fill a 16:9 frame without distorting the image. Cropping removes a portion of the top and bottom to fill the wider frame. Which is better depends on whether preserving the full image or filling the screen matters more for your use case.
